rows.v: The national bird of the United States — a huge raptor, unmistakable as an adult.
Adult: dark brown body with a brilliant white head and tail, heavy yellow beak. Wingspan up to ~2.3 m. Juveniles are mottled brown and often mistaken for golden eagles.
Near lakes, rivers, and coasts across North America, anywhere with fish and tall nesting trees. Has recovered dramatically from near-extinction.
Soars on flat, plank-like wings; surprisingly weak, chirpy call (the 'eagle scream' in movies is usually a red-tailed hawk). Steals fish from ospreys.
The most common and widespread large hawk in North America — the default roadside hawk.
Stocky, broad-winged buteo; most adults show a brick-red tail above and a dark 'belly band' across a pale chest. Variable plumage overall.
Open country, fields, deserts, and roadsides across the continent. Famously perches on highway poles scanning for prey.
Soars in wide circles; gives the iconic raspy, descending scream Hollywood dubs onto every eagle and hawk.
The fastest animal on Earth, reaching over 320 km/h in a hunting dive.
Crow-sized falcon with long pointed wings, slate-gray back, pale barred underparts, and a bold dark 'mustache' mark on the face.
Cliffs, coasts, and — increasingly — city skyscrapers worldwide, where it hunts pigeons. Recovered after DDT nearly wiped it out.
Hunts by 'stooping' — a breathtaking high-speed dive onto birds in midair. Fast, powerful, direct flight on flickering wingbeats.
A large fish-eating raptor sometimes called the 'fish hawk,' found on every continent but Antarctica.
Brown above, white below, with a white head crossed by a dark eye stripe. Long, crooked wings show a dark 'wrist' patch in flight.
Always near water — lakes, rivers, coasts, and reservoirs. Builds bulky stick nests on poles, platforms, and dead trees.
Hovers, then plunges feet-first into water to grab fish. Flies with fish held head-forward to reduce drag.
A large scavenger with an unfeathered red head, riding the wind on outstretched wings.
All dark with silvery flight feathers beneath, a small bare red head, and long broad wings. Holds wings in a shallow 'V' (dihedral).
Across the Americas in open and wooded country. Roosts communally; follows warm air currents on migration.
Rocks and tilts as it soars, rarely flapping. One of the few birds with a strong sense of smell — it finds carrion by scent.
A medium-sized woodland hawk, the classic 'backyard bird-feeder hawk' that hunts other birds.
Slate-gray back, rusty-barred chest, long banded tail with a rounded tip, and red eyes in adults. Females noticeably larger than males.
Woodlands, suburbs, and city parks across North America. Increasingly common around feeders, hunting the songbirds they attract.
Flies with several quick flaps then a glide; bursts through cover to ambush birds. Agile and acrobatic among trees.
The familiar wild duck of ponds and parks — ancestor of most domestic ducks.
Male: glossy green head, yellow bill, chestnut chest, curled black tail feathers. Female: mottled brown with an orange-and-black bill. Both show a blue wing patch.
Virtually any freshwater across the Northern Hemisphere — ponds, marshes, rivers, city parks. Extremely adaptable.
The classic loud 'quack' is actually the female; males give a quieter rasp. 'Dabbles,' tipping bottom-up to feed.
The big, honking goose seen in V-formation and on countless lawns and golf courses.
Large, with a black head and neck, a white 'chinstrap' across the cheek, brown body, and pale chest.
Across North America near water and grass; many populations no longer migrate and live year-round in parks and suburbs.
Loud, resonant honking, especially in flight. Flies in V-formations; aggressively defends nests in spring.
Often called the most beautiful duck in North America — a jewel of wooded swamps.
Male: iridescent green-and-purple crested head with bold white stripes, red eye, chestnut chest. Female: gray-brown with a white teardrop eye-ring.
Wooded swamps, marshes, and quiet ponds with trees. Unusual among ducks for nesting in tree cavities and boxes.
Ducklings leap from high nest holes to the ground days after hatching. Gives a rising squeal when flushed.
A huge, elegant white swan with an orange bill — graceful but introduced and often aggressive.
All-white with a long curved neck, orange bill with a black knob at the base. Holds neck in a graceful S-curve.
Ponds, lakes, and coastal bays; native to Eurasia but widely introduced and now feral in North America.
Despite the name, hisses, grunts, and snorts rather than being silent. Aggressively defends territory, even against people.
An iconic diving bird of northern lakes, famous for its haunting, echoing call.
Breeding adult: black head, blood-red eye, checkerboard black-and-white back, banded neck collar. Heavy, dagger-like bill.
Clear northern lakes in summer; coasts and large lakes in winter (in plainer gray plumage). A symbol of northern wilderness.
Its wailing, yodeling, and laughing calls carry across lakes at night. Dives deep to catch fish; nearly helpless on land.
The classic harbinger of spring — a familiar thrush hopping across lawns.
Gray-brown above with a warm brick-orange breast, dark head, white throat streaks, and a yellow bill.
Lawns, gardens, parks, and woodlands across North America. Among the first birds singing before dawn.
Cocks its head to 'listen' for worms, then runs and stops across the grass. Sings a cheerful, caroling 'cheerily, cheer-up'.
A beloved year-round backyard bird — the bright red 'redbird' of the eastern US.
Male: brilliant red all over with a black face mask and a pointed crest. Female: warm tan with red tinges, same crest and mask.
Woodland edges, gardens, and shrubby backyards across the eastern and central US. A common, welcome feeder visitor.
Both sexes sing a clear, whistled 'birdy-birdy-birdy' or 'cheer-cheer-cheer.' Doesn't migrate, brightening winter feeders.
A small, vivid thrush and a classic symbol of happiness, saved by nest-box programs.
Male: deep blue above with a rusty-orange throat and chest, white belly. Female: grayer with blue-tinged wings and tail.
Open country with scattered trees — meadows, orchards, and roadsides — in the eastern US. Nests in cavities and boxes.
Perches low and drops to catch insects. Gives a soft, warbling 'chur-lee' call. Recovered thanks to widespread nest boxes.
The bright 'wild canary' that brightens summer fields and thistle feeders.
Breeding male: brilliant lemon-yellow with a black cap and black-and-white wings. Female and winter birds: dull olive-yellow.
Weedy fields, gardens, and roadsides across North America. A late nester, timed to thistle and sunflower seeds.
Bounces through the air in deep undulations, calling 'po-ta-to-chip.' Almost entirely vegetarian, eating seeds.
A widespread, streaky brown sparrow with one of the most variable, musical songs.
Brown and gray with heavy dark streaks converging into a central spot on the chest. Long, rounded tail it pumps in flight.
Brushy fields, marsh edges, gardens, and thickets across most of North America. One of the continent's most common sparrows.
Sings a bright, complex song that varies by region — a few clear notes followed by a trill. Pumps its tail as it flies.
An abundant marsh blackbird whose males flash scarlet shoulder patches.
Male: glossy black with bright red-and-yellow shoulder patches (epaulets). Female: heavily streaked brown, looking like a large sparrow.
Marshes, wet fields, and roadside ditches across North America — one of the continent's most numerous birds.
Males sing a gurgling 'conk-la-ree!' from cattails and fiercely defend territory, even dive-bombing intruders.
A flame-orange songbird that weaves a hanging, sock-like nest.
Male: brilliant orange and black with white wing bars. Female: yellow-orange and grayer. Slender, pointed bill.
Open woodlands, parks, and leafy suburbs in the eastern US in summer; winters in the tropics.
Gives a rich, flutelike whistle from the treetops. Sips nectar and fruit — attracted to orange halves and jelly feeders.
The familiar all-black, highly intelligent bird with the classic 'caw.'
Entirely glossy black, including the bill and legs; fan-shaped tail in flight. Smaller than a raven, with a straighter bill.
Nearly everywhere in North America — fields, towns, woods, and dumps. Gathers in huge winter roosts.
Says a clear 'caw-caw.' Remarkably smart: uses tools, recognizes human faces, and remembers them for years.
A large, shaggy, supremely intelligent black bird of wild and mountainous country.
Bigger and bulkier than a crow, with a heavy bill, shaggy throat feathers, and a wedge-shaped (diamond) tail in flight.
Mountains, forests, deserts, and coasts across the Northern Hemisphere. Returning to many areas it was once driven from.
Gives a deep, croaking 'gronk' rather than a caw. Soars and tumbles in acrobatic flight; among the smartest of all birds.
A bold, noisy, crested blue bird that dominates eastern feeders.
Bright blue above with white underparts, a blue crest, black necklace, and white-and-black barred wings and tail.
Oak and mixed woods, parks, and suburbs in the eastern and central US. Plants oak forests by caching acorns.
Loud and varied: harsh 'jay-jay' screams, plus eerily accurate imitations of hawk calls to scare other birds off feeders.
A flashy, long-tailed black-and-white relative of crows in the open West.
Bold black-and-white pattern with iridescent blue-green wings and an extremely long, tapering tail.
Open country, ranchland, and streamside thickets in the western US and Canada. Often seen near livestock.
Noisy, chattering calls. Builds huge domed stick nests. Curious and intelligent, like its crow and jay relatives.
The smallest and most common North American woodpecker — a tiny feeder regular.
Black-and-white with a checkered back, white belly, and (in males) a small red patch on the back of the head. Tiny, stubby bill.
Woodlands, parks, and backyards across most of North America. Readily visits suet feeders.
Gives a soft 'pik' and a descending whinny. Taps lightly on small branches; nearly identical to the larger Hairy Woodpecker.
A spectacular crow-sized woodpecker with a flaming red crest — the 'Woody Woodpecker' bird.
Big and black with a bright red triangular crest, white neck stripes, and white wing flashes in flight.
Mature forests with large dead trees across much of North America. Increasingly seen in wooded suburbs.
Loud, ringing, irregular call and powerful drumming. Chisels large rectangular holes in trees hunting carpenter ants.
An unusual brown woodpecker that feeds on the ground, eating ants.
Brown with black bars and spots, a black chest crescent, and a flash of yellow or red under the wings and tail. White rump in flight.
Open woods, parks, and lawns across North America. Often seen hopping on the ground rather than on tree trunks.
Gives a loud, rolling 'wick-wick-wick' and a sharp 'klee-yer.' Drums on metal gutters in spring to claim territory.
A common eastern woodpecker, confusingly named for a faint belly tinge, not its bold red cap.
Pale gray face and underparts with a black-and-white 'zebra' back and a red crown and nape (the belly red is hard to see).
Woodlands, parks, and suburbs in the eastern US, expanding northward. A frequent feeder visitor.
Gives a rolling 'churr' call. Stores food in bark crevices; comes readily to suet and seed feeders.
A tall, stately wading bird — the largest heron in North America.
Blue-gray with a long neck, dagger bill, black eye plume, and long legs. Stands over a meter tall; flies with neck folded into an S.
Marshes, shorelines, rivers, and ponds across North America. Nests colonially in treetop 'rookeries.'
Stalks slowly in shallow water, then spears fish with a lightning strike. Gives a harsh, croaking squawk when flushed.
An enormous white waterbird with one of the largest wingspans on the continent.
Bright white with black wingtips, a huge orange bill and throat pouch. Wingspan up to ~2.7 m.
Large lakes and marshes in the interior West in summer; southern coasts in winter. Breeds in colonies on isolated islands.
Unlike brown pelicans, it doesn't dive — groups herd fish cooperatively at the surface and scoop them up. Soars in graceful formations.
The common 'seagull' of parking lots, parks, and lakes far from any sea.
White with a gray back, black wingtips, yellow legs, and a yellow bill with a distinct black ring near the tip.
Lakes, rivers, fields, and urban areas across North America — often nowhere near the ocean.
Bold and adaptable, scavenging fries and scraps. Gives the classic squealing gull calls. Takes years to reach adult plumage.
A tall gray crane famous for spectacular migrations and bugling calls.
Tall and gray with a red crown, long neck and legs, and a bustle of feathers over the rump. Flies with neck outstretched.
Marshes, prairies, and fields. Gathers by the hundreds of thousands at migration stopovers like Nebraska's Platte River.
Gives a loud, rolling, bugling call audible for miles. Performs leaping, bowing courtship dances. Flies with neck straight, unlike herons.
A stocky, big-headed bird that dives headfirst for fish.
Blue-gray with a shaggy crest, white collar, and a heavy bill. Females have an extra rusty band across the belly.
Along rivers, lakes, and coasts across North America. Nests in burrows dug into earthen banks.
Gives a loud, dry rattle as it flies along the water. Hovers, then plunges headfirst to catch fish.
A powerful, wide-ranging owl with prominent ear tufts — the classic 'hoot owl.'
Large and bulky with feathered 'horns' (ear tufts), a white throat patch, yellow eyes, and mottled gray-brown camouflage.
Forests, deserts, city parks — nearly every habitat across the Americas. One of the most adaptable owls.
Gives a deep, rhythmic 'hoo-h'HOO-hoo-hoo.' A fierce nocturnal hunter that even takes skunks and other raptors.
A round-headed, dark-eyed owl of wet woodlands, known for its 'who-cooks-for-you' call.
Stocky and gray-brown with no ear tufts, dark brown eyes (most owls have yellow), and vertical belly streaks over horizontal chest bars.
Mature, often swampy forests in the eastern US, expanding into the Pacific Northwest. Roosts quietly by day.
Famous hooting cadence sounds like 'Who cooks for you? Who cooks for you-all?' Active at dusk and on cloudy days.
A pale, ghostly, heart-faced owl found on nearly every continent.
Pale golden-buff above, white below, with a distinctive white heart-shaped facial disc and dark eyes. Long legs.
Open country, farmland, and grasslands worldwide. Nests in barns, hollow trees, and nest boxes.
Gives an eerie, raspy shriek rather than a hoot. Hunts low over fields, locating rodents by sound with extraordinary hearing.
A tiny, well-camouflaged owl with a wavering, whinnying call — not a screech.
Small with ear tufts and yellow eyes; comes in gray or rusty-red color forms. Superbly camouflaged against bark.
Wooded suburbs, parks, and forests in the eastern US. Roosts in tree cavities and nest boxes, often in plain sight.
Gives a soft, descending whinny and an even trill — not an actual screech. Strictly nocturnal and easily overlooked.
A large white owl of the Arctic, made famous as Harry Potter's Hedwig.
Big and rounded with no ear tufts; males nearly pure white, females and young flecked with dark bars. Bright yellow eyes.
Breeds on the open Arctic tundra; some winters move south into fields, airports, and shorelines ('irruptions').
Largely silent in winter. Hunts by day as well as night, unusual for an owl — adapted to the round-the-clock Arctic summer.
A tiny, fearless, endlessly cheerful feeder bird that often comes to the hand.
Round little bird with a black cap and bib, white cheeks, gray back, and buffy sides. Big head, small body.
Woods, parks, and backyards across the northern US and Canada. A constant, tame presence at feeders.
Says its own name: 'chick-a-dee-dee-dee,' adding more 'dees' when alarmed. Also whistles a clear 'fee-bee.'
The ubiquitous little brown bird of cities and farms worldwide — introduced from Europe.
Male: gray crown, black bib, chestnut nape, streaky brown back. Female: plain brown with a pale eyebrow stripe.
Cities, farms, and buildings on every inhabited continent. Thrives around human structures and activity.
Gives monotonous chirps and cheeps. Highly social, nesting in building crevices; an aggressive competitor with native birds.
A slim, soft-gray dove whose mournful cooing is one of the most familiar bird sounds.
Slender and gray-tan with black spots on the wings, a long pointed tail with white edges, and a small head.
Open country, farms, and suburbs across North America — one of the most abundant and widespread birds.
Its low, sad 'coo-OO-oo-oo-oo' is often mistaken for an owl. Wings whistle sharply on takeoff.
A tiny, dazzling hummingbird — the common breeding hummingbird of eastern North America.
Iridescent green above, pale below; males flash a brilliant ruby-red throat that can look black in poor light. Needle-like bill.
Gardens, woodland edges, and feeders in the eastern US in summer. Migrates astonishingly far for its size, crossing the Gulf of Mexico.
Hovers and even flies backward, wings humming. Fiercely territorial at flowers and nectar feeders despite its tiny size.
A compact bird famous for walking headfirst down tree trunks.
Blue-gray back, white face and underparts, black cap, and a long straight bill. Stubby tail, big head.
Mature woods, parks, and wooded backyards across North America. A regular at seed and suet feeders.
Gives a nasal 'yank-yank.' Creeps down trunks head-first, a trick that lets it spot insects other birds miss.
A small, neat sparrow nicknamed the 'snowbird' for arriving with winter.
Slate-gray or brown above with a crisp white belly, pink bill, and white outer tail feathers that flash in flight.
Breeds in northern and mountain forests; floods into backyards and feeders across the US in winter.
Gives a musical trill and sharp smacking call notes. Forages on the ground, flashing white tail feathers as it flushes.
